text: There is a wide consensus that training a deep neural network strongly needs large amounts of labeled training data, amounting to thousands of samples. Here we show that highly accurate, full-resolution, segmentation can be obtained by strongly exploiting data augmentation when only a few images are available for training. In our approach, an encoding network path is used for context learning and a symmetric decoding path for localization of the output. This network is end-to-end trainable and we demonstrate that using it we can train and apply segmentation models to train on very few samples (less than 10 images per class). On the ISBI challenge dataset for neural cell segmentation in stacks of EM images our model reaches performance figures which significantly outclass the competition winner, namely a sliding-window based CNN. Using the same base model but initially trained with samples taken from the images of the ISBI 2015 “cell tracking challenge” - which involved transmitted light microscopy images, (phase contrast and DIC) - our results of cell tracking scored first prize in the mentioned challenge in these modality categories. The network segments in about less than one second large images with modern hardware. Our full Caﬀe-based implementation including the pre-trained networks for the applied use cases are available from http://lmb.informatik.uni-freiburg.de/people/ronneber/u-net. 1
explanation: Text reads like a formal research abstract with concrete dataset references and a real URL, matching known human-authored papers on U-Net. This pattern and specificity suggest it is more likely human-written than AI-generated.
